plan.origContent:
## Scope 3 Good-Faith Protection

A reporting entity is **not subject to administrative penalties** for Scope 3 misstatements made with a reasonable basis and disclosed in good faith (§ 38532(f)(2)(B)). This is a statutory protection, not an exemption from the disclosure obligation itself.

**Effect on workspace:** For the workspace as a potential reporting entity, this means the Scope 3 disclosure carries lower legal risk than Scope 1 and Scope 2 during initial years — provided the methodology is documented and the reasonable-basis standard is met.

## Limited Penalty Window for Scope 3 (2027–2030)

Between 2027 and 2030, penalties on Scope 3 reporting are limited to nonfiling only. Misstatements (as opposed to non-filing) do not trigger penalties in this window, provided the good-faith standard is met (§ 38532(f)(2)(C)).

## Structural Flexibility for Multi-Jurisdiction Filers

The statute expressly permits reporting entities to submit reports prepared for other national or international reporting requirements (e.g., SEC climate disclosure, EU CSRD-equivalent) as long as those reports satisfy all SB 253 requirements (§ 38532(c)(1)(D)(i)). For workspaces with European operations already subject to CSRD or ESRS E1, there may be meaningful overlap — but **Legal Confirmation Required** as to whether any existing CSRD-compliant disclosure fully satisfies SB 253's specific requirements.

## Severable Provisions

If any provision of § 38532 is held invalid by a court, the remaining provisions continue in effect (§ 38532(h)). This is relevant because SB 253 has faced legal challenges; the workspace's legal team should monitor the litigation status.

**Research Gap:** The current status of any legal challenge to SB 253 was not available in the source pool for this brief and must be tracked by legal counsel independently.

*Source: § 38532(f)(2)(B), (f)(2)(C), (c)(1)(D)(i), (h).*

plan.origContent:
## The Rule

On March 29, 2024, EPA issued a final rule revising existing standards to reduce GHG emissions from heavy-duty vehicles beginning in MY 2027, and setting new, more stringent standards for MY 2028 through MY 2032. The rule was published at 89 FR 29440 on April 22, 2024, and took effect June 21, 2024.

The Phase 3 rule is the third in a series: Phase 1 covered MY 2014–2018 (issued September 2011); Phase 2 covered MY 2019–2027 (issued October 2016); Phase 3 extends and substantially tightens standards from MY 2027 onward.

*Source: EPA, "Regulations for Greenhouse Gas Emissions from Commercial Trucks & Buses," last updated February 13, 2026. https://www.epa.gov/regulations-emissions-vehicles-and-engines/regulations-greenhouse-gas-emissions-commercial-trucks

*Source: Federal Register 89 FR 29440, EPA/Office of the Federal Register, April 22, 2024. https://www.federalregister.gov/documents/2024/04/22/2024-06809/greenhouse-gas-emissions-standards-for-heavy-duty-vehicles-phase-3

## Vehicles in Scope

The rule applies to HD vocational vehicles (delivery trucks, refuse haulers, public utility trucks, transit, shuttle, and school buses) and tractors (day cabs and sleeper cabs on tractor-trailer trucks) in Classes 2b–8, representing vehicles with GVWR over 8,500 lbs up to and including the heaviest Class 8 tractor-trailers.

*Source: EPA, "Regulations for Greenhouse Gas Emissions from Commercial Trucks & Buses," last updated February 13, 2026. https://www.epa.gov/regulations-emissions-vehicles-and-engines/regulations-greenhouse-gas-emissions-commercial-trucks

## Why This Applies to the Workspace

The workspace, as a freight forwarder purchasing trucking capacity from third-party carriers for road legs across US trade corridors, is not a direct compliance subject. The direct compliance obligation falls on vehicle manufacturers and, in some provisions, operators of covered fleets.

However, four indirect effects are operationally material:

1. **Equipment cost pass-through.** Manufacturers producing MY 2027+ compliant vehicles face higher unit costs driven by ZEV drivetrain, battery, or advanced internal-combustion technology requirements. Those costs are expected to be reflected in carrier equipment pricing and, over time, in freight rates. *The magnitude of pass-through to spot or contract rates is not publicly available at the carrier-specific level and requires carrier-by-carrier commercial monitoring.*

2. **Fleet transition timing.** Carriers operating time-sensitive, high-value cargo services — the profile most relevant to live events, fine art, luxury goods, and film and TV shipments — may face procurement constraints on compliant vehicles, affecting service availability on key corridors beginning MY 2027.

3. **Corridor infrastructure dependency.** ZEV compliance by carriers operating on longer-haul routes (relevant to high-value automotive and humanitarian cargo) depends on charging and fueling infrastructure availability. The 2024 National Zero-Emission Freight Corridor Strategy identifies this as a constraint on heavy-duty ZEV adoption timelines. *Source: Joint Office of Energy and Transportation, "2024 National Zero-Emission Freight Corridor Strategy" (DOE/EE-2816). https://driveelectric.gov/files/zef-corridor-strategy.pdf

4. **Scope 3 emissions reporting exposure.** For workspaces subject to or preparing for Scope 3 emissions disclosure, the GHG intensity of road freight subcontractors is a reportable input. Phase 3 compliance by carriers will over time reduce the emissions intensity of contracted trucking, but during the fleet transition period (MY 2027–2032), the mix of compliant and non-compliant equipment in carrier fleets will affect reported Scope 3 figures.

*Source: Federal Register 89 FR 29440, EPA/Office of the Federal Register, April 22, 2024.*
*Source: EPA, "Fast Facts on Transportation Greenhouse Gas Emissions." https://www.epa.gov/greenvehicles/fast-facts-transportation-greenhouse-gas-emissions

---

plan.origContent:
## Carrier Monitoring System

**Scope:** US road carriers used for drayage, airport transfers, venue-to-venue, and port-to-warehouse legs across all cargo verticals.
**Requirement arising from Phase 3:** Not a direct compliance requirement on the workspace. A prudent operational response to the cost and availability risk the rule creates.
**Specific action:** Build a carrier monitoring log that captures, per preferred US carrier: (a) current fleet composition by MY, (b) fleet renewal timeline, (c) whether Phase 3-compliant equipment is available on specific corridors used by the workspace, (d) any communicated rate impacts.
**Gap from current baseline:** Carrier fleet data is not typically collected at this level of detail in standard freight forwarding operations. The workspace should initiate data requests.
**Deadline:** Before MY 2027 vehicle deliveries begin (calendar year 2026 production start).

## Scope 3 Data Infrastructure (Conditional)

**Scope:** Applicable only if the workspace is subject to or voluntarily pursuing GHG disclosure.
**Requirement:** Carrier-supplied activity data (tonne-kilometres, fuel type, vehicle class) or spend-based proxy data for US road freight legs.
**Gap from current baseline:** Not confirmed from available sources. **Legal Confirmation Required** as to applicable reporting framework and methodology before building this infrastructure.

## Client-Facing Sustainability Claims Protocol

**Scope:** All cargo verticals where clients request sustainability credentials or emissions data for US road legs.
**Requirement:** The workspace must not represent Phase 3 compliance status of its carriers as confirmed without verification. Given the legal uncertainty noted above, any client communication about US road freight emissions standards should be reviewed by counsel before use.
**Gap from current baseline:** No protocol confirmed; one should be established.

---

plan.origContent:
The H2Accelerate initiative does not impose operational system requirements on the workspace directly. However, it is a leading indicator for three categories of operational adjustment the workspace should build toward before 2029:

**1. Carrier data capture — fuel type and emissions intensity fields (scope: road operations; deadline: ahead of CSRD client reporting cycles, targeted build by 2027; gap: likely significant from current baseline)**
The project will generate a publicly accessible fleet performance report covering real-world hydrogen truck operating data. Once carriers begin deploying hydrogen trucks on EU lanes, the workspace's transport management system (TMS) will need to record carrier fuel type at the vehicle or fleet level — not just at the mode level — to enable accurate lane-level GHG intensity reporting. Current TMS configurations at most freight forwarders default to ICAO/GLEC methodology estimates rather than actual carrier fuel data. A field-level gap analysis against the EU transport GHG accounting regulation (COM(2023) 441) methodology should be conducted when implementing rules are published (expected 2026–2027). *Source: EC DG MOVE, COM(2023) 441 political agreement announcement, November 2025. https://transport.ec.europa.eu/news-events/news/commission-welcomes-political-agreement-new-rules-harmonise-transport-emissions-calculations-eu-2025-11-06_en.

**2. Carrier questionnaire — hydrogen deployment timeline and surcharge structure (scope: European road carrier relationships; deadline: at next contract renewal for EU road carriers; gap: no current evidence this question is embedded in standard carrier RFP or renewal processes)**
The workspace should embed two questions into its European road carrier onboarding and renewal process: (i) whether the carrier is participating in any hydrogen truck pilot programme (including H2Accelerate TRUCKS or comparable OEM-led programmes); and (ii) the carrier's projected surcharge structure for hydrogen-fuelled movements once trucks enter their fleet. This costs nothing and builds the data needed before clients begin asking. Relevant to all EU road carrier relationships covering live events, fine art, high-value automotive, luxury goods, and film and TV lanes.

**3. Commercial staff briefing on hydrogen road freight — status and timeline (scope: client-facing staff across all cargo verticals; deadline: within 60 days; gap: no sourced evidence of existing briefing material)**
Commercial staff speaking with clients on sustainability commitments must be able to accurately characterise hydrogen road freight as a late-2020s prospect. Specifically: the project deploys 125 trucks across ≥6 EU member states by project end (31 January 2029); the consortium targets series manufacturing readiness from as early as 2026 at the OEM level, with full industrialisation volumes (tens of thousands per OEM annually) targeted around 2030; eight CEF-funded refuelling stations are under development in France and the Netherlands but do not constitute a commercial network for arbitrary EU lanes. Staff should avoid claiming workspace access to hydrogen carrier capacity (no open-market access exists) or quoting specific fuel cost or gCO2e/tkm figures (not yet available from real-world operation). *Source: Clean Hydrogen Partnership Project Dashboard. https://www.clean-hydrogen.europa.eu/projects-dashboard/projects-repository/h2accelerate-trucks_en. H2Accelerate consortium site. https://h2accelerate.eu/trucks/.

**4. Intelligence calendar — project milestone and AFIR review tracking (scope: internal intelligence function; deadline: immediate; gap: not yet embedded if item is newly surfaced)**
Set recurring review triggers aligned to: (a) H2Accelerate TRUCKS mid-project reporting outputs (expected 2026–2027 via CORDIS and Clean Hydrogen Partnership); (b) AFIR regulatory review due 31 December 2026, which will determine whether the 2030 hydrogen HRS network density target remains on track; and (c) publication of EU member state NPFs for key road freight countries (France, Germany, Netherlands, Belgium, Spain, Italy). No new system build is required — this is a calendar and monitoring protocol update.

plan.origContent:
**MONITORING**

The H2Accelerate collaboration has secured two tranches of EU funding:

- **H2Accelerate TRUCKS:** A project funded by the Clean Hydrogen Partnership (a joint undertaking under Horizon Europe) to deploy 150 fuel cell electric trucks across Europe by the mid-to-late 2020s. The Clean Hydrogen Partnership confirmed a grant of €30 million for this project in a 14 March 2023 announcement.
- **H2Accelerate Inaugural Station Deployment:** A parallel project awarded funding under the Connecting Europe Facility (CEF) to deploy eight heavy-duty hydrogen refuelling stations to support the truck deployments.

*Source: Clean Hydrogen Partnership News Release, "H2Accelerate to deploy 150 fuel cell trucks across EU," 14 March 2023. https://www.clean-hydrogen.europa.eu/media/news/h2accelerate-deploy-150-fuel-cell-trucks-across-eu-2023-03-14_en (Tier 3 — Intergovernmental body communication).*

The trucks to be deployed are configured for long-haul heavy-duty operations: 4×2 or 6×2 axle configuration, up to 44-tonne capacity, with a target minimum range of 600 km per fill. Deployment involves three OEM manufacturers and one retrofitter; the vehicles will be placed with "trusted customers of the OEMs" and operated under real-world conditions over several years to assess technical and commercial performance.

*Source: Clean Hydrogen Partnership News Release, 14 March 2023 (as above).*

The trigger for this initiative is the EU's broader hydrogen strategy and the Fit for 55 package, which set binding CO2 reduction targets for heavy-duty vehicles. Separately, Regulation (EU) 2024/1610 amended the CO2 standards for new heavy-duty vehicles, tightening fleet-average emission requirements from 2030 onward, creating structural commercial pressure on road carriers to transition away from diesel.

*Source: Regulation (EU) 2024/1610 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 14 May 2024 amending Regulation (EU) 2019/1242, EUR-Lex OJ:L_202401610. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=OJ:L_202401610 (Tier 1 — Binding EU law). Regulation (EU) 2019/1242, EUR-Lex CELEX:32019R1242. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=CELEX:32019R1242 (Tier 1 — Binding EU law).*

**Cause-and-effect chain:**
- **What is happening:** EU CO2 standards for heavy-duty vehicles tighten from 2030, and funded pilot deployments are placing hydrogen trucks in commercial service by the mid-to-late 2020s. *(Sourced above.)*
- **Mechanical consequence:** Road carriers operating on EU routes face regulatory pressure to reduce fleet emissions; hydrogen trucks represent one available compliance pathway that is now under funded real-world testing with OEM-grade equipment.
- **Effect on workspace road operations:** For a freight forwarder booking road freight across Europe, hydrogen truck availability among carrier partners is a medium-term variable. The deployment timeline (trucks operating in real-world conditions through to approximately 2029 under the project term) means commercial-scale carrier access to hydrogen trucks on booked lanes is unlikely before the late 2020s at the earliest. Effect on specific cargo verticals (live events, fine art, high-value automotive) requiring premium or specialized road carriers requires carrier-specific data — hydrogen truck availability will depend on whether the workspace's road carrier partners are among the OEM customer pool in this project.

plan.origContent:
**What the workspace can credibly say:**

- "The EU has funded a 150-truck hydrogen fuel cell deployment project running through to 2029. This is a real-world testing programme, not commercial deployment. Series manufacturing is the goal for the second half of this decade."
- "Hydrogen trucks on European lanes are a late-2020s commercial prospect, not a current operational option."
- "The workspace monitors this initiative because our European road freight carrier base will be subject to the EU's tightening HDV CO2 standards from 2030, and hydrogen is one pathway carriers may use to comply."
- "We can discuss the expected emissions intensity advantages of hydrogen road freight for your Scope 3 reporting once real-world performance data from the initiative becomes available — that data is expected to be an output of the project by its 2029 end date."

**What to avoid saying:**

- Do not claim the workspace has access to hydrogen truck capacity. The project deploys vehicles with OEM-selected customers; no evidence supports open-market access.
- Do not cite specific hydrogen fuel costs or gCO2e/tkm reduction figures — these are not yet sourced from real-world operation of this fleet.
- Do not conflate the eight-station CEF project with a sufficient refuelling network for arbitrary European lanes. Eight stations is a pilot infrastructure, not a commercial network.
- Do not state that any specific carrier partner of the workspace is participating in the deployment — this requires carrier-specific confirmation.

**Questions the workspace can pose to road carrier partners:**
- Are you or your fleet operators participating in the H2Accelerate TRUCKS deployment or in any hydrogen truck pilot programme?
- What is your projected timeline for hydrogen truck availability on [specific lane]?
- How will hydrogen fuel costs be reflected in your surcharge structures when hydrogen trucks enter your fleet?

---

- Do not claim the workspace has access to hydrogen truck capacity. The project deploys vehicles with OEM-selected customers; no evidence supports open-market access.
- Do not cite specific hydrogen fuel costs or gCO2e/tkm reduction figures — these are not yet sourced from real-world operation of this fleet.
- Do not conflate the eight-station CEF project with a sufficient refuelling network for arbitrary European lanes. Eight stations is a pilot infrastructure, not a commercial network.
Operational implication: - Do not state that any specific carrier partner of the workspace is participating in the deployment — this requires carrier-specific confirmation.

**Questions the workspace can pose to road carrier partners:**
- Are you or your fleet operators participating in the H2Accelerate TRUCKS deployment or in any hydrogen truck pilot programme?
- What is your projected timeline for hydrogen truck availability on [specific lane]?
- How will hydrogen fuel costs be reflected in your surcharge structures when hydrogen trucks enter your fleet?

plan.origContent:
**What the NFSP Is**

The National Freight Strategic Plan is a federal planning instrument required under 49 U.S.C. § 70102 (as amended by the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act, Pub. L. 117-58, November 2021). DOT is required to develop and update a multimodal freight strategic plan that addresses the condition and performance of the national multimodal freight network, identifies strategies to improve that network, and incorporates sustainability and resilience goals. The plan itself does not impose regulatory obligations on private freight operators. It informs federal infrastructure grant priorities (including INFRA grants, Port Infrastructure Development Program grants, and Multimodal Project Discretionary Grants), state freight planning requirements, and DOT internal investment decisions.

The 2025 update cycle has been initiated through a formal RFI published July 15, 2025, in which DOT is soliciting public input to inform the updated plan's content and priorities. *Source: Federal Register / U.S. Department of Transportation, 2025-13219, July 15, 2025.*

The 2020 NFSP (the current operative version pending the 2025 update) was published in September 2020 and established five goals: safety, economic competitiveness, equity, sustainability, and resilience. *Source: U.S. Department of Transportation, "2020 National Freight Strategic Plan," September 2020. https://www.transportation.gov/sites/dot.gov/files/2020-09/NFSP_fullplan_508.pdf

**Why It Applies to the Workspace**

As a freight forwarder, the workspace does not hold direct obligations under the NFSP. However, three indirect mechanisms are operationally relevant:

1. **Infrastructure investment shaping:** NFSP priorities influence which multimodal corridors receive federal infrastructure investment, which affects road and intermodal drayage options on the workspace's Americas trade lanes. For cargo verticals including high-value automotive, live events, and humanitarian where time-definite road segments are critical, corridor investment decisions influence routing options over a 3–7 year horizon.

2. **Zero-emission freight corridor alignment:** The 2024 National Zero-Emission Freight Corridor Strategy (published jointly by DOE/DOT/EPA) explicitly frames ZEF corridor deployment in alignment with NFSP priorities. The NFSP 2025 update is expected to incorporate or reference ZEF corridor goals, which will shape where zero-emission drayage becomes operationally available — directly relevant to clients in the live events and fine art verticals who are beginning to impose carrier sustainability requirements on their forwarders. *Source: Joint Office of Energy and Transportation, "2024 National Zero-Emission Freight Corridor Strategy," DOE/EE-2816. https://driveelectric.gov/files/zef-corridor-strategy.pdf

3. **Comment window:** The RFI constitutes the formal public input phase for the 2025 NFSP. Freight forwarders with multimodal US operations can file comments that directly shape prioritization language — including language around specialty cargo handling, time-critical freight, and road network resilience — that then appears in a federal planning document referenced by state DOTs and grant reviewers.

---

plan.origContent:
**COST ALERT** — EU ETS aviation surcharges are live and carrier-pass-through is already occurring on intra-EEA and departing flights. For a workspace routing live events, fine art, and luxury goods primarily by air across Europe, every air shipment touching an EU ETS-covered aviation segment carries an embedded carbon cost. The workspace must establish whether carriers are itemising this surcharge separately or absorbing it into base rate. If it is not itemised, the workspace cannot report it to clients or reclaim it in commercial discussions.

- **Action:** Within 30 days, request from each air carrier used on Europe trade lanes a breakdown of whether EU ETS aviation costs are included in base rate or quoted as a separate line item. Document the response.
- **Who is affected:** All cargo verticals moving by air through the EU/EEA, primarily live events, fine art, luxury goods, film and TV.
- **Why now:** EU ETS aviation scope has been in force since 2012 for intra-EEA flights; full-scope flights and maritime were expanded. Carrier pass-through is active. Not knowing the embedded cost means the workspace cannot price, report, or negotiate accurately.

*Source: ICAP ETS Map — EU ETS entry, ICAP, 2025–2026. https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/ets.

---

**COST ALERT** — EU ETS maritime scope is in force and expanding. Ocean shipments touching EU ports carry EU ETS costs for vessel operators. For the workspace's tertiary ocean volume (e.g., heavy automotive, large-format live events equipment), carriers on EU port-calling routes are surrendering allowances, and cost pass-through is occurring industry-wide.

- **Action:** Within 30 days, confirm with each ocean carrier on Europe trade lanes whether EU ETS maritime surcharges are itemised on invoices. If not itemised, flag for commercial and finance teams.
- **Who is affected:** High-value automotive and large-format live events cargo moved by ocean to/from EU ports.
- **Why now:** EU ETS maritime entered Phase 1 in 2024 (50% of emissions on qualifying voyages). Carrier pass-through is in market. This is not a future cost; it is a current one.

*Source: ICAP ETS Map — EU ETS entry, ICAP, 2025–2026. https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/ets.

---

**MONITORING** — California Cap-and-Trade and Washington Cap-and-Invest apply to fuel distributors supplying transport fuel on those trade lanes. These costs flow through to road carriers and, on some ocean routes, vessel operators bunkering in-state. For the workspace's Americas road movements (e.g., last-mile delivery of high-value automotive or live events equipment in California or Washington), embedded carbon costs are present in trucking rates.

- **Action:** No immediate compliance action required. Flag for rate renegotiation cycle: ask road carriers serving California and Washington whether carbon cost pass-through is embedded in line-haul rates.
- **Who is affected:** High-value automotive, live events cargo moving by road in California or Washington.
- **Why now:** California program has been in force since 2013 (current allowance price USD 29.10 in 2025); Washington since 2023 (current price USD 61.36 in 2025 — the highest sub-national price in the Americas). These are current costs, not anticipated ones.

*Source: ICAP ETS Map — USA California and USA Washington entries, ICAP, 2025–2026. https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/ets.

---

**MONITORING** — Japan GX-ETS covers maritime, domestic aviation, and transport sectors and is in force. The workspace's Japan-routed air and road movements face carrier-level compliance costs under this system.

- **Action:** In the next 60 days, request Japan-operating air and road carriers to confirm whether GX-ETS costs are passed through in rates on Japan trade lanes.
- **Who is affected:** Live events, luxury goods, fine art, film and TV cargo transiting Japan by air or road.
- **Why now:** GX-ETS is in force. Specific allowance price not published in available ICAP data for this entry; effect on the workspace's Japan corridor requires carrier-specific data.

*Source: ICAP ETS Map — Japan GX-ETS entry, ICAP, 2025–2026. https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/ets.

---

**MONITORING** — Korea ETS (K-ETS) covers maritime, domestic aviation, transport, and other sectors. Korea is a significant trade lane for luxury goods and high-value automotive. Allowance price in 2025: KRW 9,393 (USD 6.60) on secondary market.

- **Action:** Monitor K-ETS pricing cycle. At next carrier contract review for Korea routes, confirm pass-through status.
- **Who is affected:** Luxury goods, high-value automotive on Asia (Korea) lanes.

*Source: ICAP ETS Map — Korea ETS entry, ICAP, 2025–2026. https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/ets.

---

**MONITORING** — EU ETS 2 (covering road transport and buildings) is under development, covering all 30 EU/EEA member states. If enacted as proposed, it will impose costs directly on road fuel distributors serving EU trucking, feeding through to road carrier rates across the workspace's European road movements.

- **Action:** Track EU ETS 2 legislative progress. No compliance action required now, but road carrier contracts in Europe should include a carbon cost review clause before EU ETS 2 enters force.
- **Who is affected:** All cargo verticals using road transport within the EU.

*Source: ICAP ETS Map — EU ETS 2 entry, ICAP, 2025–2026. https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/ets.

---

plan.origContent:
MLIT is Japan's lead ministry for land, sea, and air transport infrastructure and policy. Its Maritime Bureau holds regulatory authority over international and coastal shipping, seafarer certification, vessel safety, port-state control, and environmental compliance for shipping operating in Japanese waters.

The Maritime Bureau's English portal confirms Japan's participation as a founding member of an international framework to support shipping with technology challenges, and confirms Japan's public commitment to a net-zero GHG emissions target for global shipping by 2050 — a position Japan stated at IMO MEPC in October 2021.

*Source: MLIT Maritime Bureau English portal, MLIT, accessed April 2025. [https://www.mlit.go.jp/en/maritime/index.html](https://www.mlit.go.jp/en/maritime/index.html)*

**Why this applies to the workspace:** Workspaces operating as freight forwarders on Asia trade lanes — including Japan origin or destination routing — are affected by MLIT Maritime Bureau regulations in two ways. First, ocean freight carriers calling Japanese ports are subject to Japanese port-state control (PSC) inspections, vessel safety standards, and environmental reporting requirements that flow through to service quality, vessel availability, and routing decisions. Second, Japan's stated alignment with IMO's 2050 net-zero strategy means Japanese-flag vessels and port infrastructure will progressively face tightening requirements consistent with IMO MEPC.377(80), the 2023 IMO GHG Strategy.

*Source: IMO Resolution MEPC.377(80), 2023 IMO Strategy on Reduction of GHG Emissions from Ships, adopted 7 July 2023. [https://wwwcdn.imo.org/localresources/en/KnowledgeCentre/IndexofIMOResolutions/MEPCDocuments/MEPC.377(80).pdf](https://wwwcdn.imo.org/localresources/en/KnowledgeCentre/IndexofIMOResolutions/MEPCDocuments/MEPC.377(80).pdf)*

For the workspace's highest-priority verticals — live events, fine art, high-value automotive, and humanitarian — ocean freight is tertiary by volume but is used for large, heavy, or cost-sensitive consignments. Disruption to Japanese port operations or vessel routing driven by regulatory tightening has a direct effect on lead times and routing options for these shipments.

---

**MONITORING — Japan–IMO 2050 Net-Zero Alignment, Ocean Routing Review**

- **Action:** Review ocean freight carrier panel for Japan-connected routes and confirm which carriers hold or are pursuing compliance posture consistent with IMO MEPC.377(80). This is directly relevant to tertiary ocean mode for high-value automotive and large live events cargo routed via Japan.
- **Cost:** Effect on freight rates from IMO-aligned fuel transition is not quantifiable from available sources at this time. Directionally, Japanese-flag and Japanese-port-calling carriers are expected to face compliance cost increases as IMO CII ratings tighten post-2026. *Cost impact requires carrier-specific data.*
- **Who is affected:** Operations and commercial leads managing Japan-origin or Japan-transit ocean freight bookings across all verticals.
- **Why now:** Japan has publicly aligned with net-zero 2050 at IMO and participates in the international framework for shipping technology challenges. Carrier compliance differentiation on Japan routes will become visible in rate structures and service availability ahead of IMO enforcement milestones. Early carrier conversations provide lead time.

*Source: MLIT Maritime Bureau portal, MLIT, April 2025; IMO MEPC.377(80), July 2023.*

---

**MONITORING — GX-ETS and Road Freight Capacity Claims: Research Gap**

The existing brief preview contains specific claims about Japan's GX Emissions Trading Scheme (mandatory from 2026, 100,000-tonne threshold) and the 2024 truck driver overtime cap ("2024 Problem"). These claims are **not grounded in the provided source content** (the MLIT Maritime Bureau landing page). They may be accurate but require verification against primary MLIT policy documents or authoritative secondary sources before the workspace relies on them operationally or in client conversations.

- **Action:** Retrieve and verify the GX-ETS legislative instrument and the MLIT 2nd Basic Plan on Transport Policy (available at the MLIT policy document URL in the available sources pool) before using GX-ETS timelines in client communications or supply chain planning.
- **Who is affected:** Commercial leads using Japan decarbonisation claims in client conversations; operations leads planning road freight capacity on Japan-connected inland movements.

plan.origContent:
The following definitional questions determine whether and how the Act applies. Each is marked as **Decided** (answerable from the bill text) or **Legal Confirmation Required** (not resolved by the text or any published agency guidance as of the date of this brief).

**Q1: Does "operates within the state" require California domicile, physical presence, or merely conducting business in California?**
→ **Legal Confirmation Required.** The bill text does not define "operates within the state." No California Attorney General guidance or CARB guidance addressing this question has been published as of the date of this brief.

**Q2: Does making a sustainability claim on a website accessible in California — without any California office or operations — trigger Section 44475.2?**
→ **Legal Confirmation Required.** The text of Section 44475.2(c) exempts entities that "do not operate within the state." Whether a website accessible in California constitutes "making claims within the state" is unresolved.

**Q3: Does the definition of "voluntary carbon offset" in Section 44475(d)(3)(A) cover offset-backed freight products sold to clients?**
→ **Legal Confirmation Required.** The statutory definition covers "any product sold or marketed in the state that claims to be a greenhouse gas emissions offset…or any like term." Whether a "carbon neutral shipment" product meets this definition depends on how it is described in the workspace's commercial materials.

**Q4: Does Section 44475.1's disclosure obligation apply to offsets purchased in prior years that were cited in claims made after January 1, 2024?**
→ **Legal Confirmation Required.** The Act does not specify a retroactivity period. No enforcement guidance has been published.

**Q5: Are "significant reductions" claims — without using the words "carbon neutral" or "net zero" — covered by Section 44475.2?**
→ **Decided by the text.** Section 44475.2 explicitly covers "other claims implying the entity…has made significant reductions to its carbon dioxide or greenhouse gas emissions." Claims such as "we have reduced our freight emissions by X%" or "our shipments are lower-carbon" are potentially within scope if they imply significance. **Legal Confirmation Required** as to where the threshold of "significance" lies.

**Q6: Is the disclosure obligation satisfied by a PDF, or must it be on a live website page?**
→ **Decided by the text.** All three sections require disclosure "on the entity's internet website." A PDF linked from the website likely satisfies this, but a PDF distributed only by email or in an RFP response does not. **Legal Confirmation Required** for confirmation by counsel.

*Source: AB 1305, California Health and Safety Code §§ 44475–44475.3, California Legislature, October 7, 2023. https://leginfo.legislature.ca.gov/faces/billTextClient.xhtml?bill_id=202320240AB1305

---

plan.origContent:
## Carbon Tax (Green Economy Pillar — Energy Reset)

Singapore's carbon tax is the most operationally material Green Plan instrument for the workspace's supply chain. The tax is levied on facilities emitting 25,000 tCO₂e or more per year. Covered entities include power generation and industrial facilities, not freight forwarders directly.

**Carbon tax trajectory (legislatively scheduled):**
- 2024: S$25/tCO₂e — **in force**
- 2026: S$45/tCO₂e — **scheduled**
- 2030: S$50–80/tCO₂e — **target range**

The mechanism by which regulated carriers and handlers pass this cost into freight rates is commercial, not statutory — meaning the workspace has negotiating room but no regulatory lever to require transparency.

*Source: ICAP Allowance Price Explorer, https://icapcarbonaction.com/en/ets-prices (Tier 1). World Bank Carbon Pricing Dashboard, https://carbonpricingdashboard.worldbank.org (Tier 3). Statutory confirmation of specific threshold and rate figures from the Singapore Carbon Pricing Act text is a **research gap** due to source access failure.*

**Effect by cargo vertical:**
- *Live events, film and TV:* High volume, time-critical shipments through Singapore as a regional hub. Carbon cost pass-through on air freight is lower in absolute terms per shipment (high value, low weight) but surcharge line items are more visible to cost-sensitive production budgets.
- *Fine art, luxury goods:* Typically low-weight, high-value air freight. Absolute carbon surcharge impact per shipment is low; reputational alignment with carrier sustainability credentials is commercially relevant for client sustainability messaging.
- *High-value automotive:* Ocean-tertiary shipments of larger components or vehicles transiting Singapore face higher absolute carbon cost exposure per TEU.
- *Humanitarian:* Rate sensitivity is high; carbon pass-through surcharges directly affect delivered cost to humanitarian programme operators. Carrier selection and surcharge transparency are critical.

## Maritime Singapore Green Initiative — Green Ship Programme

The Green Ship Programme provides incentives to Singapore-registered vessels meeting defined emissions reduction standards. The 2024 revisions expanded eligible technologies and revised criteria.

For the workspace, this matters through ocean carrier selection: carriers operating Singapore-registered vessels under the programme may have different cost structures (incentive-offset versus non-incentive) reflected in freight rates.

*Source: MPA, "No. 12 of 2024 – Revisions to the Maritime Singapore Green Initiative – Green Ship Programme," https://www.mpa.gov.sg/media-centre/details/no.-12-of-2024---revisions-to-the-maritime-singapore-green-initiative---green-ship-programme-for-singapore-registered-ships (Tier 1, regulator).*

**Effect on ocean-tertiary operations:** Requires carrier-specific data. The workspace should request Green Ship Programme enrollment status from ocean carriers on Singapore-transiting lanes.

## IMO GHG Strategy — Interface with Singapore

Singapore's maritime decarbonisation commitments operate in parallel with the IMO's 2023 GHG Strategy (Resolution MEPC.377(80)), which targets net-zero shipping emissions by or around 2050. Singapore is an IMO member state and aligns its port-level requirements with IMO milestones. The IMO strategy's CII (Carbon Intensity Indicator) rating system for vessels affects which vessels call at Singapore and at what cost.

*Source: IMO, Resolution MEPC.377(80) – 2023 IMO Strategy on Reduction of GHG Emissions from Ships (adopted 7 July 2023), https://wwwcdn.imo.org/localresources/en/KnowledgeCentre/IndexofIMOResolutions/MEPCDocuments/MEPC.377(80).pdf (Tier 1, binding IMO resolution).*

**Effect on ocean-tertiary operations:** Vessels with poor CII ratings face operational restrictions that may affect schedule reliability and cost on Singapore-transiting routes. Effect on specific workspace verticals requires carrier-specific CII data.

---

plan.origContent:
Direct registration obligations under Singapore's Carbon Pricing Act fall on facility-level emitters above the 25,000 tCO₂e threshold. Freight forwarders operating in Singapore as service intermediaries (without large physical facilities) are unlikely to be direct registrants under the carbon tax — **but this requires legal confirmation under Singapore law.**

**Indirect reporting obligation — client Scope 3 data:** EU-headquartered clients subject to CSRD, or clients with published net-zero commitments, may contractually require the workspace to provide Singapore-leg emissions data. The workspace's ability to fulfil this depends on:
1. Carrier-level emissions disclosure quality (Singapore legs, air and ocean)
2. Whether the workspace has an emissions accounting framework that captures Singapore-transiting movements

No Singapore regulation directly requires freight forwarders to generate or provide per-shipment emissions data to clients as of the date of this brief — **but this is an active commercial exposure independent of regulatory obligation.**

*Source: MSE, "Singapore Green Plan 2030," https://www.mse.gov.sg/resources/sgp-2030/ (Tier 2). Legal Confirmation Required for direct registration threshold confirmation.*

**ACTION REQUIRED — Establish ISO 14083-compliant emission calculation capability before 1 July 2025 (CountEmissions EU application date).**

For a freight forwarder on European trade lanes, ISO 14083-aligned emission certificates issued per shipment will be a legal requirement under CountEmissions EU from 1 July 2025. The workspace must either implement calculation software in-house, integrate with a certified third-party calculation platform, or pass the obligation contractually to carriers with confirmation of compliance. Each pathway requires lead time. The window for implementation without disruption to client reporting cycles is narrowing.

- **Action:** Map which shipments touch EU origin or destination by trade lane (Americas–Europe, Europe–Asia) and quantify the volume requiring compliant certificates from 1 July 2025.
- **Action:** Contact primary air carriers on European lanes and request confirmation of ISO 14083-compliant emissions factor data availability. Carrier-specific emission factors are preferred under the standard; default factors are permitted only where carrier-specific data is unavailable. Carrier data readiness varies and is not uniformly confirmed. *[Effect on air vertical requires carrier-specific data.]*
- **Action:** Evaluate calculation platform options (e.g., EcoTransIT World, CargoHub, Searoutes, or equivalent). Cost of third-party platforms is not publicly standardised; current rates require direct vendor enquiry.
- **Action:** Brief commercial team on ISO 14083 compliance status ahead of any live events, fine art, luxury goods, high-value automotive, or film and TV tenders in European markets. Legal Confirmation Required: whether the workspace's existing carrier contracts require amendment to compel ISO 14083 data provision.

plan.origContent:
## Emissions Calculation System

**Scope:** All shipments on EU-touching trade lanes (Americas–Europe, Europe–Asia) from 1 July 2025. Recommended: all shipments globally to serve non-EU clients with equivalent reporting needs (Americas, Asia lanes).
**Deadline:** 1 July 2025 (CountEmissions EU application date).
**Gap from current baseline:** If the workspace is currently calculating emissions using spreadsheet-based tools or industry average default factors without a structured ISO 14083 allocation and certificate workflow, the gap is significant: the system must support per-shipment multi-leg calculation, allocation method selection and disclosure, data quality tier recording, and certificate generation. Building or integrating this capability typically requires 3–6 months depending on system complexity, carrier data onboarding, and IT integration. No proprietary timeline data is available; this range is an operational assessment based on publicly described platform implementation requirements, not a sourced figure.

**Specific gap for specialist verticals:** Live events and fine art frequently use charter air and specialist trucking. These operators may not have ISO 14083-compliant emissions data. The workspace should identify, by vertical, which carriers lack compliant factor data and implement default-value fallbacks with disclosure, while committing to upgrade to carrier-specific data as it becomes available.

## Carrier Data Onboarding

**Scope:** All active carriers across air, road, and ocean modes.
**Deadline:** Prior to 1 July 2025.
**Gap:** Carriers vary in readiness. A structured data request process — specifying ISO 14083 data quality tier required, the format needed, and the integration method — must be designed and executed. This is a procurement and supplier management task, not solely an IT task.

## Client Communication System

**Scope:** All clients who receive freight invoices or who include emissions reporting in contractual requirements.
**Deadline:** Before 1 July 2025 for EU-touching clients; immediately for RFP processes already in market.
**Gap:** The workspace must be able to include ISO 14083-compliant emission certificates in the standard shipment documentation pack, either as an attachment or via client portal access. If current systems do not generate this document type, this is a system gap.

## Staff Training

**Scope:** Operations, commercial, and customer service teams.
**Content required:** What ISO 14083 requires, how the calculation system works, what clients are entitled to request, how to handle vertical-specific edge cases (charter air for live events and film/TV, fine art consolidated shipments, humanitarian-route default factors).
**Deadline:** Before 1 July 2025; recommended prior to any 2025 tender submissions.

*Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1805 (CountEmissions EU), Articles 3, 6, 10. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=CELEX%3A32023R1805

plan.origContent:
**MONITORING — Track SAF cost pass-through trajectory linked to NZIA manufacturing targets.**
The NZIA sets an explicit objective to expand EU SAF production capacity to meet ReFuelEU Aviation blending mandates. The blending mandate under ReFuelEU Aviation begins at 2% SAF in 2025 and escalates through 2050. The NZIA's permitting streamlining is designed to accelerate the EU manufacturing base for SAF. However, the gap between current EU SAF production capacity and the mandate trajectory is well-documented; carriers are already pricing SAF surcharges on air freight. For the workspace — air primary — the rate of NZIA-enabled capacity expansion will determine whether SAF surcharge costs stabilise or escalate over the 2025–2030 period. Assign a staff member to monitor NZIA Strategic Project designations related to SAF production facilities and cross-reference against carrier SAF surcharge communications. No immediate compliance action required. Cost monitoring required now.

*Source: Regulation (EU) 2024/1735, Recital 16; OJ L 2024/1735, 28 June 2024. Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/2405, Article 4 (blending mandates), OJ, 18 October 2023.*

**MONITORING — Assess whether any cargo client in technology, energy equipment, or automotive verticals is subject to NZIA procurement sustainability criteria.**
Clients in the high-value automotive vertical or technology-adjacent sectors who are EU contracting authorities or entities procuring net-zero technology products face mandatory sustainability and resilience criteria in procurement documents. If such clients are sourcing equipment via the workspace from Asia, they may face new documentation obligations on origin and supply-chain concentration. The workspace should ask: do any existing clients procure net-zero technology products under EU public procurement rules? If yes, a documentation capability question arises. No action is required until a client-side trigger is confirmed. Legal Confirmation Required as to whether the workspace's documentation obligations change when acting as forwarder for cargo subject to NZIA procurement criteria.

*Source: Regulation (EU) 2024/1735, Recitals 55–58; OJ L 2024/1735, 28 June 2024.*

**MONITORING — Watch CO₂ transport cargo category development.**
The NZIA's 50 Mt/year CO₂ injection capacity target by 2030, combined with Strategic Project designation for CO₂ capture and transport infrastructure, will generate new cargo flows in liquid CO₂, CCS equipment, and related components. CO₂ transport is explicitly described as using ships, barges, trains, and trucks. For the workspace's road and ocean modes, this represents a potential new cargo vertical. No operational change is required now. Identify whether the workspace has dangerous goods handling capability for liquefied or compressed CO₂ (UN 1013, UN 2187 class categories) and flag internally for commercial development tracking.

*Source: Regulation (EU) 2024/1735, Recitals 36, 47–48; OJ L 2024/1735, 28 June 2024.*

plan.origContent:
**Q1: Does the workspace have any obligation to register, report, or notify under the NZIA?**
**Decided: No.** The NZIA's obligations fall exclusively on project promoters, contracting authorities, auction designers, and oil and gas production licensees. Freight forwarders are not addressed as a regulated entity type. No registration, notification, or reporting obligation exists for the workspace under this regulation.

*Source: Regulation (EU) 2024/1735, operative provisions; OJ L 2024/1735, 28 June 2024.*

**Q2: Does the 50% single-country supply concentration trigger create any documentation obligation for the workspace as forwarder?**
**Legal Confirmation Required.** The regulation's resilience mechanism (Recitals 57–58, 62, 68) triggers procurement document conditions when a single third country supplies more than 50% of a net-zero technology or its main specific components within the Union. This obligation falls on contracting authorities, not on freight forwarders. However, if the workspace is asked by a client to provide origin certificates or supply-chain documentation to support the client's compliance with procurement conditions, the question of accuracy, liability, and professional duty in providing such documentation requires legal review under applicable EU customs and trade law.

*Source: Regulation (EU) 2024/1735, Recitals 57–58, 62, 68; OJ L 2024/1735, 28 June 2024.*

**Q3: Is CO₂ cargo covered by any special regime under the NZIA that affects the workspace's dangerous goods obligations?**
**Legal Confirmation Required.** The NZIA designates CO₂ transport infrastructure as strategic and supports transport via ships, barges, trains, and trucks. It does not itself alter dangerous goods classification or carriage rules. However, as CO₂ transport cargo flows develop, the workspace should confirm with relevant competent authorities and its insurance carriers that its existing dangerous goods certifications cover liquid and compressed CO₂ under the applicable ADR (road), IMDG (ocean), and IATA DGR (air) regimes. This is not an NZIA compliance question but is triggered by the NZIA-enabled cargo category.

*Source: Regulation (EU) 2024/1735, Recital 47; OJ L 2024/1735, 28 June 2024.*

**Q4: Does the NZIA's reference to sustainable fuels manufacturing create any direct SAF purchasing obligation for the workspace?**
**Decided: No.** The NZIA's SAF provisions target fuel producers and the manufacturing scale-up environment. SAF blending obligations are imposed on aircraft fuel suppliers under ReFuelEU Aviation (Regulation (EU) 2023/2405), not on freight forwarders. The workspace's SAF exposure is cost pass-through via carrier pricing, not a direct obligation.

*Source: Regulation (EU) 2024/1735, Recital 16; Regulation (EU) 2023/2405, OJ, 18 October 2023.*

**IRENA is not a regulation.** It is an intergovernmental organization with 168 member states and the European Union. Its stated mission is to serve as "the principal platform for international co-operation, a centre of excellence, and a repository of policy, technology, resource and financial knowledge on renewable energy." *Source: IRENA, "Vision and Mission — IRENA Statute," irena.org (id: 13ec22b2-4391-4c5a-9643-fa0c0fd8542e).*

IRENA does not impose compliance obligations on freight forwarders, logistics operators, or any private-sector entity directly. It produces analytical work that informs member state policy, and member states may — at their own discretion — translate IRENA recommendations into domestic regulation or procurement requirements. Any operational impact on the workspace flows through that downstream translation step, not from IRENA itself.

**Why this item was flagged for the workspace:** The workspace operates through the UAE/Middle East corridor, and IRENA's Abu Dhabi headquarters places it at the center of the UAE's renewable energy and clean economy policy environment. The UAE's broader clean energy posture — including the Dubai Clean Energy Strategy 2050, which targets 100% clean energy for Dubai by 2050 — is directly informed by the policy environment IRENA operates within. *Source: UAE Government (u.ae), "Dubai Clean Energy Strategy," u.ae (id: 9a5fa993-83d2-4278-be3e-eaafa90231e2).* Whether this UAE policy posture produces binding freight-sector obligations is a downstream question that requires monitoring of UAE domestic regulatory instruments, not IRENA outputs directly.

**Direct applicability to the workspace's compliance chain:** None established at this time. IRENA issues no instrument that creates a compliance obligation for freight forwarders on any of the workspace's active trade lanes (Americas, Europe, Asia). The Middle East and Africa (MEAF) jurisdiction tag on this item reflects IRENA's host country, not a regulatory jurisdiction that binds freight operators.

plan.origContent:
*Note: Direct substantive requirements under ACT fall on truck manufacturers, not freight forwarders. This section describes the manufacturer requirements and their operational consequences for the workspace as a carrier-dependent forwarder.*

## Manufacturer ZEV Sales Targets (the direct ACT obligation)

Beginning with model year 2024, manufacturers certifying Class 2b–8 combustion-engine trucks for California sale must meet escalating ZEV sales percentages. The targets ramp annually through 2035. The 2035 endpoint targets are: 55% for Class 2b–3, 75% for Class 4–8 straight trucks, and 40% for truck tractors.

- **Cause:** Manufacturer non-compliance exposes OEMs to CARB enforcement.
- **Mechanical consequence:** Manufacturers are incentivized to shift production mix toward ZEV units; diesel truck availability in California (and adopting states) will progressively tighten relative to ZEV supply.
- **Effect on the workspace's road operations:** Drayage carriers and over-the-road carriers serving the workspace's California freight — across live events, luxury goods, fine art, high-value automotive, film/TV, and humanitarian verticals — will face a structurally changing used and new truck market. Effect on carrier pricing and availability requires carrier-specific data not publicly available as of April 2026.

*Source: CARB Advanced Clean Trucks program page. https://ww2.arb.ca.gov/our-work/programs/advanced-clean-trucks

## One-Time Fleet Reporting Requirement

ACT contains a one-time reporting requirement for large entities and fleets operating in California. CARB's program page directs fleet-related inquiries to the Advanced Clean Fleets program page. The reporting scope, threshold definitions, and deadline for this requirement are governed by ACF, not ACT.

*Source: CARB Advanced Clean Trucks program page. https://ww2.arb.ca.gov/our-work/programs/advanced-clean-trucks

**Research gap:** The specific threshold definitions and compliance timeline for the ACT large-entity/fleet reporting requirement are not fully resolved from the ACT program page alone. The workspace should cross-reference the ACF regulation text and CARB's ACF compliance guidance to determine applicability.

## Credit and Flexibility Mechanisms

CARB's program page references "Pooling Amendments" as an active rulemaking item, indicating that manufacturers have access to credit mechanisms that provide flexibility in meeting annual ZEV sales targets. The substantive terms of the pooling mechanism are not resolved in the available source pool.

*Source: CARB Advanced Clean Trucks program page (references "Pooling Amendments" notice of public hearing). https://ww2.arb.ca.gov/our-work/programs/advanced-clean-trucks

*Note: Direct substantive requirements under ACT fall on truck manufacturers, not freight forwarders. This section describes the manufacturer requirements and their operational consequences for the workspace as a carrier-dependent forwarder.*

## Manufacturer ZEV Sales Targets (the direct ACT obligation)

Beginning with model year 2024, manufacturers certifying Class 2b–8 combustion-engine trucks for California sale must meet escalating ZEV sales percentages. The targets ramp annually through 2035. The 2035 endpoint targets are: 55% for Class 2b–3, 75% for Class 4–8 straight trucks, and 40% for truck tractors.

- **Cause:** Manufacturer non-compliance exposes OEMs to CARB enforcement.
- **Mechanical consequence:** Manufacturers are incentivized to shift production mix toward ZEV units; diesel truck availability in California (and adopting states) will progressively tighten relative to ZEV supply.
- **Effect on the workspace's road operations:** Drayage carriers and over-the-road carriers serving the workspace's California freight — across live events, luxury goods, fine art, high-value automotive, film/TV, and humanitarian verticals — will face a structurally changing used and new truck market. Analytical inference: Effect on carrier pricing and availability requires carrier-specific data not publicly available as of April 2026.

*Source: CARB Advanced Clean Trucks program page. https://ww2.arb.ca.gov/our-work/programs/advanced-clean-trucks

## One-Time Fleet Reporting Requirement

ACT contains a one-time reporting requirement for large entities and fleets operating in California. CARB's program page directs fleet-related inquiries to the Advanced Clean Fleets program page. The reporting scope, threshold definitions, and deadline for this requirement are governed by ACF, not ACT.

*Source: CARB Advanced Clean Trucks program page. https://ww2.arb.ca.gov/our-work/programs/advanced-clean-trucks

**Research gap:** The specific threshold definitions and compliance timeline for the ACT large-entity/fleet reporting requirement are not fully resolved from the ACT program page alone. The workspace should cross-reference the ACF regulation text and CARB's ACF compliance guidance to determine applicability.

## Credit and Flexibility Mechanisms

CARB's program page references "Pooling Amendments" as an active rulemaking item, indicating that manufacturers have access to credit mechanisms that provide flexibility in meeting annual ZEV sales targets. The substantive terms of the pooling mechanism are not resolved in the available source pool.

*Source: CARB Advanced Clean Trucks program page (references "Pooling Amendments" notice of public hearing). https://ww2.arb.ca.gov/our-work/programs/advanced-clean-trucks

plan.origContent:
## Plain-language summary

MARPOL Annex VI is the binding international treaty controlling air pollution from ships. It was adopted in 1997 and has been substantially revised through successive MEPC resolutions, most recently via the 2021 Revised MARPOL Annex VI (Resolution MEPC.328(76)), which entered into force 1 November 2022.

The annex operates through six principal regulatory tracks:

1. **Sulphur oxide (SOx) limits** — global 0.50% m/m fuel sulphur cap (in force since 1 January 2020); 0.10% m/m within designated Emission Control Areas (ECAs). Ships may alternatively use exhaust gas cleaning systems (scrubbers) achieving equivalent emission reductions.
2. **Nitrogen oxide (NOx) standards** — Tier I, II, and III limits applied to marine diesel engines by build date and operating area. Tier III applies in designated NOx ECAs.
3. **Energy Efficiency Design Index (EEDI)** — minimum energy efficiency standard for new ships by vessel type and size, with phased reduction factors.
4. **Energy Efficiency Existing Ship Index (EEXI)** — one-time efficiency certification for existing ships; in force from 1 January 2023 certification cycle.
5. **Carbon Intensity Indicator (CII)** — annual operational carbon intensity rating (A through E) for ships above 5,000 GT; in force from 1 January 2023, with corrective action plans triggered by D or E ratings.
6. **IMO Data Collection System (DCS)** — mandatory fuel oil consumption reporting for ships above 5,000 GT, in force since 2019.

*Source: IMO Prevention of Air Pollution from Ships page, IMO.org. [https://www.imo.org/en/ourwork/environment/pages/air-pollution.aspx] — Intergovernmental body position. Source: IMO EEXI and CII FAQ, IMO.org. [https://www.imo.org/en/mediacentre/hottopics/pages/eexi-cii-faq.aspx] — Intergovernmental body position.*

## Why it applies to the workspace

The workspace, operating as a freight forwarder, is not a named obligated party under MARPOL Annex VI. The regulation does not impose registration, reporting, or compliance certification requirements on forwarders.

The workspace's exposure arises through three channels:

**Channel 1 — Cost pass-through (ocean-tertiary lanes).** Carrier compliance with EEXI, CII, and sulphur limits imposes operating cost on vessel operators. Those costs — fuel switching to VLSFO or LSFO, scrubber capital and operating expense, slow steaming to improve CII ratings, or speed optimisation — are commercially passed to cargo customers through freight rates and surcharges. The workspace, when arranging ocean freight on Americas–Europe, Americas–Asia, or Europe–Asia lanes for any of its cargo verticals, absorbs or passes on those costs. The magnitude of pass-through is carrier- and lane-specific; current rate is not publicly available in the provided source pool and requires carrier-specific data.

**Channel 2 — Carrier qualification for sustainability-sensitive cargo.** For cargo verticals where clients or end-clients require documented sustainability credentials (live events, luxury goods, film and TV productions with scope 3 commitments, automotive OEM clients), carrier CII ratings and DCS data become relevant to carrier selection decisions. A carrier operating with a D or E CII rating for two consecutive years is required under MARPOL Annex VI to submit a corrective action plan to its flag administration. That signal is publicly visible and relevant to the workspace's carrier vetting process on ocean lanes.

**Channel 3 — Scope 3 emissions reporting exposure.** If the workspace or its clients are subject to emissions disclosure frameworks (CSRD, ISSB, or voluntary GHG Protocol Scope 3), the emissions intensity of ocean legs must be quantified. CII ratings and DCS data are the primary vessel-level data sources for this calculation. For humanitarian cargo verticals where donor reporting includes emissions, and for luxury/automotive clients with public net-zero commitments, this channel is commercially material.

*Source: IMO MARPOL Convention overview, IMO.org. [https://www.imo.org/en/about/conventions/pages/international-convention-for-the-prevention-of-pollution-from-ships-(marpol).aspx] — Intergovernmental body position. Source: IMO EEXI and CII FAQ. [https://www.imo.org/en/mediacentre/hottopics/pages/eexi-cii-faq.aspx]*

plan.origContent:
**Q1: Does MARPOL Annex VI apply to the workspace directly?**
Decided: No. The regulation does not name freight forwarders as obligated parties. Obligations fall on shipowners, operators, flag state administrations, and port states. *Source: IMO MARPOL Convention overview.*

**Q2: Does the workspace's role as arranger of ocean freight create any compliance obligation under Annex VI?**
Decided (subject to legal confirmation): No direct compliance obligation is created by the forwarder role. **Legal Confirmation Required** in any jurisdiction that has enacted domestic legislation extending Annex VI-adjacent obligations (carbon levy collection, reporting mandates) to cargo arrangers.

**Q3: Does the Mediterranean Sea ECA designation affect the workspace's ocean lanes?**
Decided: It affects carrier operating costs on Europe-touching ocean lanes via Mediterranean routing (in force 1 May 2024). Rate impact requires carrier-specific data. *Source: MEPC.361(79).*

**Q4: Do CII ratings affect which carriers the workspace can use?**
Decided: No regulatory restriction prevents a forwarder from booking cargo on a D- or E-rated vessel. The relevance is commercial and reputational, not compliance-based. For sustainability-sensitive cargo verticals (luxury, automotive, humanitarian), client contracts or policy commitments may create effective restrictions. **Legal Confirmation Required** for any client contract clause that references carrier emissions ratings as a booking condition.

**Q5: Does the DCS data improvement from MEPC.385(81) (in force 1 August 2025) change the workspace's Scope 3 calculation options?**
Decided: Yes, to the extent the workspace uses vessel-level IMO DCS data to calculate ocean leg emissions for client reporting. Enhanced transport work granularity improves per-tonne-kilometre intensity calculations. The workspace's current Scope 3 methodology would need to be reviewed to determine whether DCS data is already being used or should be incorporated. *Source: MEPC.385(81).*

plan.origContent:
This document covers MARPOL Annex VI — the air pollution prevention annex to the International Convention for the Prevention of Pollution from Ships — as it applies to a freight forwarding operation whose cargo verticals include live events, fine art, luxury goods, film and TV, high-value automotive, and humanitarian goods, with air as the primary transport mode, road secondary, and ocean tertiary, operating across Americas, Europe, and Asia trade lanes.

**Convention on naming:** The workspace is referenced throughout as "the workspace" or "workspaces in the freight forwarding role." No individual or company name appears.

**Scope of this document:** The document covers the currently enforced MARPOL Annex VI obligations and the amendment pipeline through 2027, as established by the IMO MEPC resolution index. It does not cover MARPOL Annexes I through V (oil, noxious liquids, harmful substances in packaged form, sewage, garbage). It does not cover the EU's parallel maritime emissions regulations (FuelEU Maritime, EU ETS for shipping), which are distinct instruments requiring separate analysis.

**Critical framing note:** MARPOL Annex VI imposes obligations directly on shipowners, vessel operators, flag state administrations, and port state authorities — not on freight forwarders as a compliance class. The workspace's exposure is indirect: through cost pass-through from ocean carriers, through client-facing sustainability reporting obligations, and through carrier qualification decisions on ocean-tertiary lanes. This document filters every provision through that indirect exposure lens.

**Date convention:** All "in force" dates are entry-into-force dates as published in the IMO MEPC resolution index. *Source: IMO MEPC Resolutions and Guidelines Index, IMO.org. [https://www.imo.org/en/OurWork/Environment/Pages/Index-of-MEPC-Resolutions-and-Guidelines-related-to-MARPOL-Annex-VI.aspx]*

plan.origContent:
**COST ALERT — Survey European road freight subcontractors on ZEV transition plans and surcharge structures.**
Action: Initiate a structured questionnaire to road freight subcontractors operating on European legs for live events, fine art, film and TV, luxury goods, high-value automotive, and humanitarian cargo. The questionnaire should establish: (a) current diesel-to-ZEV fleet ratio; (b) planned ZEV acquisition timeline; (c) whether the carrier intends to introduce a "green transition surcharge" and on what basis; (d) charging infrastructure access on routes used for the workspace's cargo. This intelligence is needed before contract renewals, not after.
Cost: Internal procurement resource time; no external cost. Effect is on road freight rate exposure across all European cargo verticals served.
Why now: The 2030 target (-45% from baseline under the amended regulation) is the first major fleet-forcing milestone. Carriers planning to meet it must be ordering or financing ZEV trucks now, given typical 3–5 year vehicle procurement and financing cycles. Surcharge structures are being set in the market before the workspace has visibility.

**COST ALERT — Review and update multi-year road freight contracts to include ZEV transition cost provisions.**
Action: Legal Confirmation Required. Work with legal counsel to assess whether current road freight subcontract templates contain provisions that address fleet-transition cost pass-through. Where they do not, consider adding clauses that either (a) cap green-transition surcharges at a defined ceiling indexed to a public benchmark, or (b) require advance notice and mutual agreement before surcharges are applied. This is contract risk management, not compliance.
Cost: Legal review cost (current rate not publicly available; depends on counsel and contract volume). Effect is on margin protection across European road freight operations.
Why now: Window is closing on renewal cycles. Contracts already signed without these provisions will be subject to carrier-initiated renegotiation as ZEV costs materialise.

**MONITORING — Identify European road routes where electric trucks are already operationally viable.**
Action: Map the workspace's regular European road freight routes by distance and corridor. Routes under approximately 200–400 km (within current BEV truck range depending on payload and terrain) and serving urban delivery or drayage functions (e.g., venue access in major European cities, airport-to-venue legs) are candidates where subcontractors may already be deploying BEV trucks. Early identification allows the workspace to proactively market electric-truck capability on these legs to sustainability-focused clients in live events, luxury goods, and film and TV verticals.
Cost: Internal operations resource time.
Why now: Some European carriers have begun BEV truck deployments. Knowing which routes have coverage provides a commercial differentiation narrative at no additional cost.

plan.origContent:
## What the Regulation Does

Regulation (EU) 2019/1242 sets fleet-wide CO2 emission performance targets for manufacturers of new heavy-duty vehicles (HDVs) registered in the EU. It does not directly regulate who operates trucks or how they are loaded. Its obligations fall on vehicle manufacturers — the persons responsible for submitting new HDV data under Regulation (EU) 2018/956 or, for zero-emission HDVs, the type-approval holder.

The original Regulation set two reduction targets against a reference CO2 baseline (average emissions recorded 1 July 2019 to 30 June 2020):
- **−15% by the reporting period of 2025 onwards**
- **−30% by the reporting period of 2030 onwards**

These are expressed in grams of CO2 per tonne-kilometre (g/tkm), and apply to four vehicle sub-groups covering rigid lorries and tractor units above 16 tonnes (N2 and N3 categories), which account for approximately 65–70% of all HDV CO2 emissions in the EU fleet.

*Source: Regulation (EU) 2019/1242, Article 1 and Recital 9; EUR-Lex / Official Journal of the European Union, OJ L 198, 25.7.2019. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=CELEX:32019R1242

Amending Regulation (EU) 2024/1610 strengthened these targets significantly, extending coverage and tightening reduction requirements beyond 2030.

*Source: Regulation (EU) 2024/1610 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 14 May 2024; EUR-Lex / Official Journal of the European Union. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=OJ:L_202401610

The Commission publishes manufacturer performance data annually by 30 April (Article 11), making compliance records publicly visible.

*Source: Regulation (EU) 2019/1242, Article 11; EUR-Lex / Official Journal of the European Union.*

## Why This Applies to the Workspace

The workspace does not manufacture vehicles and therefore has **no direct regulatory obligation** under this Regulation. However, the Regulation operates as an indirect structural force on the workspace's road transport operations through two channels:

**Channel 1 — Fleet renewal cost pass-through:** Manufacturers achieving fleet-wide CO2 reduction are driven toward deploying zero- and low-emission HDVs. The cost of those vehicles — battery-electric trucks, hydrogen fuel cell trucks — is currently materially higher than diesel equivalents. Road transport carriers and subcontractors operating in the EU will face fleet investment obligations or excess-emissions premiums (€4,250 per gCO2/tkm excess 2025–2029, rising to €6,800 per gCO2/tkm from 2030), which they will seek to pass through to freight buyers, including freight forwarders who contract road capacity on behalf of clients.

*Source: Regulation (EU) 2019/1242, Article 8(1); EUR-Lex / Official Journal of the European Union.*

**Channel 2 — Client sustainability reporting:** Freight forwarders increasingly provide emissions data to shippers for their own Scope 3 reporting. The CO2 performance of the road fleet used — measurable via the VECTO-derived values now required by Article 12 — becomes part of the emissions data chain that clients must disclose.

For the workspace's road-secondary mode serving European trade lanes (live events touring, fine art and luxury goods ground transport, film and TV equipment moves, high-value automotive delivery), the transition of the EU truck fleet directly affects the cost and emissions profile of ground haulage subcontractors.

The workspace's primary mode (air) and tertiary mode (ocean) are governed by separate instruments (ReFuelEU Aviation, FuelEU Maritime) and are not within scope of this Regulation.

*Source: European Commission – Climate Action (DG CLIMA), "Lorries, buses and coaches – CO2 emission standards for heavy-duty vehicles." https://climate.ec.europa.eu/eu-action/transport-decarbonisation/road-transport/lorries-buses-and-coaches_en

*Source: Regulation (EU) 2024/1610 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 14 May 2024; EUR-Lex / Official Journal of the European Union. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=OJ:L_202401610

The Commission publishes manufacturer performance data annually by 30 April (Article 11), making compliance records publicly visible.

*Source: Regulation (EU) 2019/1242, Article 11; EUR-Lex / Official Journal of the European Union.*

## Why This Applies to the Workspace

The workspace does not manufacture vehicles and therefore has **no direct regulatory obligation** under this Regulation. However, the Regulation operates as an indirect structural force on the workspace's road transport operations through two channels:

**Channel 1 — Fleet renewal cost pass-through:** Manufacturers achieving fleet-wide CO2 reduction are driven toward deploying zero- and low-emission HDVs. The cost of those vehicles — battery-electric trucks, hydrogen fuel cell trucks — is currently materially higher than diesel equivalents. Road transport carriers and subcontractors operating in the EU will face fleet investment obligations or excess-emissions premiums (€4,250 per gCO2/tkm excess 2025–2029, rising to €6,800 per gCO2/tkm from 2030), which they will seek to pass through to freight buyers, including freight forwarders who contract road capacity on behalf of clients.

*Source: Regulation (EU) 2019/1242, Article 8(1); EUR-Lex / Official Journal of the European Union.*

**Channel 2 — Client sustainability reporting:** Freight forwarders increasingly provide emissions data to shippers for their own Scope 3 reporting. Operational implication: The CO2 performance of the road fleet used — measurable via the VECTO-derived values now required by Article 12 — becomes part of the emissions data chain that clients must disclose.

For the workspace's road-secondary mode serving European trade lanes (live events touring, fine art and luxury goods ground transport, film and TV equipment moves, high-value automotive delivery), the transition of the EU truck fleet directly affects the cost and emissions profile of ground haulage subcontractors.

The workspace's primary mode (air) and tertiary mode (ocean) are governed by separate instruments (ReFuelEU Aviation, FuelEU Maritime) and are not within scope of this Regulation.

*Source: European Commission – Climate Action (DG CLIMA), "Lorries, buses and coaches – CO2 emission standards for heavy-duty vehicles." https://climate.ec.europa.eu/eu-action/transport-decarbonisation/road-transport/lorries-buses-and-coaches_en

**MONITORING**

The Fit for 55 package is the European Commission's coordinated set of legislative proposals to reduce EU net greenhouse gas emissions by at least 55% by 2030 compared to 1990 levels, as required by the European Climate Law, with a pathway to climate neutrality by 2050. The package is not a single regulation. It is the policy architecture that produced, or is producing, the following component instruments directly relevant to freight forwarding operations:

- **EU ETS extension to maritime** — emissions allowance obligations for vessels calling at EU ports
- **FuelEU Maritime** — greenhouse gas intensity limits on energy used by ships at EU ports and on EU voyages, entering force from 2025
- **ReFuelEU Aviation** — sustainable aviation fuel (SAF) blending mandates on fuel suppliers at EU airports, in force from 2025
- **CBAM (Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism)** — carbon price on imports of specified goods entering the EU, transitional phase in force since October 2023
- **CO₂ standards for heavy-duty vehicles (HDVs)** — binding fleet-average emission standards for manufacturers of trucks and coaches, with compliance years from 2025 onward
- **AFIR (Alternative Fuels Infrastructure Regulation)** — mandatory deployment targets for EV charging and hydrogen/LNG refuelling infrastructure across the EU road network
- **PPWR (Packaging and Packaging Waste Regulation)** — requirements on recyclability, recycled content, and EPR for packaging placed on the EU market
- **CSRD/ESRS** — corporate sustainability reporting obligations, applying to large companies and, through supply-chain provisions, to their suppliers

*Source: COM(2021) 550 final, European Commission, 14 July 2021, Sections 2.2.1–2.2.3.*
*Source: Council of the European Union — Fit for 55 overview. https://www.consilium.europa.eu/en/policies/fit-for-55/

**Why this applies to the workspace:** The workspace, as a freight forwarder moving cargo across EU-connected air, road, and ocean lanes, sits inside the compliance chain of at least four of these instruments directly (ReFuelEU Aviation, FuelEU Maritime, EU ETS maritime, and HDV CO₂ standards as a purchaser of road freight capacity), and faces indirect exposure through CBAM, CSRD supply-chain data requests, and PPWR when facilitating imports to the EU or commissioning packaging on behalf of clients. The package as a whole is the source of simultaneous compliance pressure across all three transport modes — understanding the architecture explains why the workspace cannot treat these as unrelated events.

---

This document provides a workspace-anchored intelligence brief on the EU Fit for 55 package as the overarching policy architecture governing EU freight sustainability obligations. The source document is the European Commission Communication COM(2021) 550 final, published 14 July 2021, which introduced the package and described its structure and policy logic. That Communication is not itself a binding regulation; it is the Commission's framing document for the legislative proposals that followed.

This brief does not attempt to cover every component regulation of the Fit for 55 package in full — each component is a standalone regulation with its own compliance calendar and is addressed in separate intelligence items. The function of this brief is to explain why multiple regulations appear simultaneously, how they interlock, which components apply to the workspace's operations across air, road, and ocean modes, and what the workspace must have mapped and acted on at the package level.

The workspace operates as a freight forwarder serving live events, fine art, luxury goods, film and TV, high-value automotive, and humanitarian cargo verticals, with air as the primary transport mode, road secondary, and ocean tertiary, across Americas, Europe, and Asia trade lanes.

*Source: COM(2021) 550 final, European Commission, 14 July 2021. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=CELEX:52021DC0550
*Source: Council of the European Union — Fit for 55 overview. https://www.consilium.europa.eu/en/policies/fit-for-55/
